explicit_text: I started out listening to Joe Oliver play at the Dreamland Cafe and the Peek-In Cafe when I was just a kid. He had a unique style, especially with his little mute that created wonderful effects, which inspired me to try playing the cornet that way. I followed him to the Lincoln Gardens and even got to sit in with his band, which was rare for a white musician at the time. Joe's music and style deeply influenced me during my early years in Chicago.
implicit_text: I started out listening to a great cornet player at the Dreamland Cafe and the Peek-In Cafe when I was just a kid. I followed him to the Lincoln Gardens and even got to sit in with his band, which was rare for a white musician at the time. His music and style deeply influenced me during my early years in Chicago.

explicit_text: The Dreamland Cafe on 36th and State was one of the first places I heard Joe Oliver play. They had matinee dances there, and I used to ditch school just to go listen. The music started late at night, but the atmosphere and the sounds from Joe’s band left a lasting impression on me. It was a key spot in my early exposure to jazz in Chicago.

explicit_text: Before Joe Oliver played at the Dreamland, he was at the Peek-In Cafe. I was too young to go inside then, so I would stand outside just to listen to the music. It was a tough time for a kid like me, but that place was important because it was where I first heard Joe Oliver’s band. It marked the beginning of my fascination with jazz.

explicit_text: After the Dreamland, Joe Oliver moved to the Lincoln Gardens, and I followed him there. I was about 13 years old, still playing drums, and I would put on my brother’s long pants to sneak in and listen. That place was another important venue where I absorbed the jazz style that shaped my playing. It was a big part of my musical education in Chicago.

explicit_text: When the Dixieland band started making records, I bought every one I could find and played them on my victrola while practicing my cornet. That music was a big influence on me, and it helped me develop my style. It was exciting to play along with those recordings and learn from them. Dixieland was really the foundation of what I wanted to do musically.
implicit_text: When the traditional jazz band started making records, I bought every one I could find and played them on my victrola while practicing my cornet. That music was a big influence on me, and it helped me develop my style. It was exciting to play along with those recordings and learn from them. That style was really the foundation of what I wanted to do musically.

explicit_text: I met the fellows from the New Orleans Rhythm Kings at Friar's Inn, and I hung around with them a lot. Their style, along with Joe Oliver's, really affected me because it was different from the sweet music that was popular then. The Rhythm Kings were the best band put together at the time, and I admired how they played. It was a privilege to be around them and learn from their musicianship.
implicit_text: I met the fellows from a well-known New Orleans style band at a popular Chicago club, and I hung around with them a lot. That band was the best put together at the time, and I admired how they played. It was a privilege to be around them and learn from their musicianship.

explicit_text: Friar's Inn was a central spot where I met the New Orleans Rhythm Kings and Bix Beiderbecke. We would all gather there to listen to the band and share our love for jazz. It was a place where musicians of different backgrounds came together, and I formed some important friendships there. Friar's Inn was a hub for the Chicago jazz scene in those days.

explicit_text: Tommy Ladnier was a cornet player I admired greatly. He took Louis Armstrong's place with Joe Oliver and played second trumpet when Armstrong went to New York. Ladnier had a style that really stood out, and I looked up to him as one of the best musicians of that era. His playing influenced the way I approached my own cornet work.
implicit_text: There was a cornet player I admired greatly who took the place of a legendary musician with the great cornet player’s band and played second trumpet when that legend went to New York. He had a style that really stood out, and I looked up to him as one of the best musicians of that era. His playing influenced the way I approached my own cornet work.

explicit_text: Louis Armstrong was a huge influence on me and the whole jazz world. I remember when he left Joe Oliver's band to join Fletcher Henderson in New York. We would meet at the Sunset Cafe and listen to him play alongside Earl Hines. Armstrong even came to see me in the hospital once and gave me a hard time about drinking. He was a tough but important figure in my life and music.

He was a tough but important figure in my life and music.

explicit_text: Fletcher Henderson was the bandleader Louis Armstrong joined when he went to New York. That move was significant because it opened new opportunities for Armstrong and influenced the jazz scene nationally. While I didn't play with Henderson, his band was part of the broader context of jazz evolution that affected all of us in Chicago and beyond.

explicit_text: I met Bix Beiderbecke at Friar's Inn where the New Orleans Rhythm Kings played. We connected naturally as cornet players and even played together on the South Side at a place with just a piano and a drum. We decided to be a cornet team because our styles blended so well. Bix was a great musician and a good friend during those early Chicago days.

explicit_text: The South Side of Chicago was where Bix and I went to play together, often dropping into small places with just a piano and drum. It was a vibrant area for jazz and where many musicians gathered to jam and develop their styles. I spent a lot of time there honing my craft alongside other musicians. The South Side was a key part of my musical journey.

explicit_text: Jeff Pfeffer taught me how to pitch when I was crazy about baseball during the war. I wanted to be a pitcher before I got seriously interested in music. He was part of the Navy team with the big leaguers, and learning from him was a big deal for me. Though I eventually chose music, Jeff's coaching was an important part of my youth.

explicit_text: Ted Lewis was a wonderful guy and a bandleader I worked for. He liked hot music even if he couldn’t play it himself. He hired me and wanted my full name for the wire, which was John Francis Joseph Thomas Julian. Ted brought in great musicians like Jack Teagarden, Fats Waller, and the Dorsey Brothers to record with him. Working with Ted was a big part of my career, and he was always supportive despite the headaches I gave him.

explicit_text: Jimmy Boon was one of the musicians we liked to listen to when Ted and I went out on 10th and Wabash. We enjoyed the wine places there and the music scene. Jimmy was part of the vibrant jazz community that influenced us. Those nights out were important breaks from the grind of playing and rehearsing.

explicit_text: 10th Street in Chicago, especially around Wabash Avenue, was a favorite place for Ted and me to go out and enjoy music and wine. We’d listen to musicians like Jimmy Boon there and soak up the atmosphere. It was a lively part of the city’s nightlife and jazz culture. Those outings helped me connect with the local music scene and unwind.

explicit_text: I got a small job in Forest Park, Illinois, after playing at the Midway. It was one of the stepping stones in my career before moving on to bigger gigs. Forest Park gave me a chance to keep playing and improving my craft. It was part of my early professional journey in the Chicago area.

explicit_text: After Forest Park, I played at Terry Gardens, which was another important venue for me. Jess Stacey was with Joe Kayser at the time, and I tried to get Ted Lewis in the band there, but they wouldn’t have him. That was frustrating, but Terry Gardens was part of the circuit that helped me grow as a musician. It was a place where I kept pushing forward.

The College Inn was part of the Chicago jazz circuit, and playing there helped me connect with other musicians and audiences. It was a valuable stop on my musical path.

explicit_text: The Sunset Cafe was a big night spot where Louis Armstrong, Earl Hines, and I would meet and listen to each other play. It was a central hub for Chicago jazz and a place where musicians gathered to share ideas. Friday nights there were especially important for networking and enjoying the music scene. The Sunset Cafe was a cornerstone of my musical life in Chicago.

He was a fantastic trombonist and a big name in jazz at the time. Playing with him helped me grow as a musician and gave me exposure to a wider audience.

Waller was a master of stride piano and brought a lot of energy to the sessions. Being around him was inspiring and helped me appreciate the breadth of jazz styles.

explicit_text: Don Veremeichik was one of the most underrated musicians of our time. He was the son of a minister and once got a hose treatment from his dad for coming home drunk. Don studied harmony for four years and really knew his music. He was part of Ted Lewis’s band and brought a lot of skill and depth to our performances.

explicit_text: Artie Shaw was one of the guys who followed me around, especially when I was in Paris. He was a big name in jazz and swing, and I appreciated his style. We crossed paths during my career, and it was always good to have that kind of recognition from fellow musicians. Shaw represented the broader jazz world I was part of.

explicit_text: One night I was listening to the Chicago Symphony Orchestra when the arabesque man took up a solo that was the most beautiful thing I ever heard. That experience showed me how classical music could inspire jazz musicians like myself. It was a reminder of the musical possibilities beyond the jazz scene. The orchestra’s performance left a strong impression on me.

The orchestra’s performance left a strong impression on me.

explicit_text: I got a request to play 'There'll Be Some Changes Made,' but the person couldn’t play Ted Lewis’s part. I believe musicians should play their own way rather than copying exactly. That tune was popular, and people wanted to hear Ted’s version, but I always thought it was better to bring your own style. It’s a classic that connects to the jazz tradition I was part of.
